title: GA4 Analytics Engineering with R, Arrow & DuckDB & Parquet
text: A practical guide to downloading, storing, and analyzing Google Analytics 4 event data using modern R tools — from BigQuery extraction to high-performance local analysis.

title: GA4 Analytics Engineering with R, Arrow & DuckDB & Parquet
text:
Tools
R · Quarto · bigrquery · arrow · duckdb · dplyr · purrr

title: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
text:
1.1 Small GA4 Project (< 7 days)
For your current 7-day project, RDS alone is enough. As you scale up or start wanting SQL-style queries, DuckDB + Parquet is the most powerful pairing — DuckDB queries the Parquet files directly without even needing to load them first.


1.1.1 A few things worth highlighting from the table:
The nested column problem is the deciding factor for GA4 specifically. RDS and DuckDB both handle event_params, device, and geo as-is, while raw Parquet forces you to flatten them first (which is where all that purrr complexity came from).
DuckDB + Parquet is the best long-term investment because DuckDB can query Parquet files on disk directly with a single line — duckdb::duckdb_read_csv() or FROM read_parquet('data/*.parquet') — meaning you get the storage efficiency of Parquet without writing Arrow query pipelines.
For your current 7-day project, RDS wins on simplicity. But if you plan to scale to months of GA4 data, switching to DuckDB early costs very little effort and will save you a lot of pain later.

title: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
text:
1.2 Large GA4 Data (90 days)


1.2.1 The three key shifts from the 7-day analysis are:
RDS is now off the table. At 3 months, you’re likely looking at 3–10M rows and 500MB–2GB of raw data. Loading that entirely into RAM on every session is slow, risks crashing R with an out-of-memory error, and gives you no ability to query just a slice of the data.
DuckDB alone becomes marginal. A .duckdb file is a single binary blob — you can’t selectively read January without touching February and March. It’s fine for medium data but loses its edge at this scale.
DuckDB + Parquet is the clear winner, and the setup is simpler than it sounds. You save once as date-partitioned Parquet files, then DuckDB reads them directly on disk:
```{r}
library(duckdb)
con <- dbConnect(duckdb())

# DuckDB reads Parquet files directly — no loading into R first
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT
    event_date,
    event_name,
    ep.value.string_value AS page_location
  FROM read_parquet('data/ga4_parquet/**/*.parquet', hive_partitioning = true)
  CROSS JOIN UNNEST(event_params) AS ep(key, value)
  WHERE event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210131'
    AND ep.key = 'page_location'
")
```






Three things make this powerful at your scale: the hive_partitioning = true flag means DuckDB skips entire month folders when your WHERE clause doesn’t need them; UNNEST handles the nested GA4 columns without any upfront flattening; and nothing is loaded into R memory until dbGetQuery() returns the final filtered result.

title: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
section: 2.2 Step 2: Download from BigQuery once, save as partitioned Parquet
text:
2.2 Step 2: Download from BigQuery once, save as partitioned Parquet
Do this once only. All future analysis reads from local Parquet files.
```{r}
library(bigrquery)
library(arrow)
library(dplyr)

billing_project <- "your-project-id"

# Download in monthly chunks to avoid memory pressure
months <- list(
  c("20210101", "20210131"),
  c("20210201", "20210228"),
  c("20210301", "20210331")
)

dir.create("data/ga4_parquet", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

for (m in months) {
  message("Downloading ", m[1], " to ", m[2], "...")
  
  result <- bq_project_query(billing_project, glue::glue("
    SELECT
      event_date, event_timestamp, event_name,
      event_params, user_pseudo_id,
      device, geo, traffic_source, ecommerce, items
    FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_*`
    WHERE _TABLE_SUFFIX BETWEEN '{m[1]}' AND '{m[2]}'
  "))
  
  chunk <- bq_table_download(result, bigint = "numeric", page_size = 1000)
  
  # Write directly to partitioned Parquet — Arrow infers event_date partition
  chunk |>
    group_by(event_date) |>
    write_dataset("data/ga4_parquet", format = "parquet")
  
  rm(chunk); gc()  # free memory between chunks
}
```

Your folder structure will look like:

data/ga4_parquet/
  event_date=20210101/part-0.parquet
  event_date=20210102/part-0.parquet
  ...

title: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
section: 2.3 Step 3: Connect DuckDB to the Parquet files
text:
2.3 Step 3: Connect DuckDB to the Parquet files
At the start of every future session, just run this — no re-downloading, no loading into RAM:
```{r}
library(duckdb)
library(DBI)

con <- dbConnect(duckdb())

# Register the entire partitioned dataset as a virtual table
dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE VIEW ga4 AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet(
    'data/ga4_parquet/**/*.parquet',
    hive_partitioning = true
  )
")
```
From here, ga4 behaves like a database table. DuckDB reads only the Parquet partitions your query needs.

title: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
text:
2.4 Step 4: Query as needed
Flat columns — standard SQL:
```{r}
# Only touches January partitions on disk
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT event_date, event_name, COUNT(*) AS n
  FROM ga4
  WHERE event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210131'
  GROUP BY 1, 2
  ORDER BY 3 DESC
")
```
Nested columns — use UNNEST in SQL:
```{r}
# Extract page_location from event_params without any upfront flattening
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT
    event_date,
    user_pseudo_id,
    ep.value.string_value AS page_location
  FROM ga4
  CROSS JOIN UNNEST(event_params) AS ep(key, value)
  WHERE event_name = 'page_view'
    AND ep.key = 'page_location'
    AND event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210131'
")
```
Device and geo nested fields:
```{r}
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT
    device.category AS device_type,
    device.operating_system AS os,
    geo.country,
    geo.city,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_pseudo_id) AS users
  FROM ga4
  WHERE event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210331'
  GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4
  ORDER BY 5 DESC
")
```
Purchase funnel — joining unnested params:
```{r}
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT
    event_name,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_pseudo_id) AS users,
    COUNT(*)                        AS events
  FROM ga4
  WHERE event_name IN ('session_start','view_item','add_to_cart','purchase')
    AND event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210331'
  GROUP BY 1
  ORDER BY 3 DESC
")

```

title: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
section: 2.5 Step 5: Close the connection when done
text:
2.5 Step 5: Close the connection when done
```{r}
dbDisconnect(con)
```

title: GA4 BigQuery Analysis with Arrow & DuckDB
text:
Code
```{r}
#| label: install-packages
#| eval: false

# Run once to install required packages
install.packages(c(
  "bigrquery", # BigQuery connector
  "DBI", # Database Interface
  "dplyr", # Data manipulation
  "arrow", # Apache Arrow (columnar, efficient)
  "duckdb", # In-process OLAP SQL engine
  "ggplot2", # Plotting
  "lubridate", # Date handling
  "scales", # Axis formatting
  "glue" # String interpolation
))
```




Code
```{r}
#| label: load-packages

library(bigrquery)
library(DBI)
library(dplyr)
library(arrow)
library(duckdb)
library(tidyverse)
```

title: GA4 BigQuery Analysis with Arrow & DuckDB
text:
2a. Date range helper


Code
```{r}
#| label: date-range

# Define a rolling 90-day window
#date_end   <- Sys.Date() - 1                         # yesterday
#date_start <- date_end - 89                          # 90 days back

date_end <- as.Date("2021-01-31")
date_start <- as.Date("2021-01-01")

# Format as YYYYMMDD for BQ table suffix pattern
bq_start <- format(date_start, "%Y%m%d")
bq_end <- format(date_end, "%Y%m%d")
```

title: GA4 BigQuery Analysis with Arrow & DuckDB
section: 2b. BigQuery SQL — flatten GA4 nested structs
text:
2b. BigQuery SQL — flatten GA4 nested structs

GA4 data is heavily nested (events → event_params, items, etc.). We unnest the key fields we need in SQL before pulling.



Code
```{r}
#| label: bq-sql
library(glue)

sql <- glue(
  "
  SELECT
    -- Session / user dims
    PARSE_DATE('%Y%m%d', event_date) AS event_date,
    user_pseudo_id,
    (SELECT value.int_value
       FROM UNNEST(event_params)
      WHERE key = 'ga_session_id') AS session_id,

    -- Traffic source
    traffic_source.source AS source,
    traffic_source.medium AS medium,
    traffic_source.name AS campaign,

    -- Device
    device.category AS device_category,
    device.operating_system AS os,

    -- Geo
    geo.country AS country,

    -- Event
    event_name,
    event_timestamp,

    -- Ecommerce (NULL for non-purchase events)
    ecommerce.purchase_revenue AS revenue,
    ecommerce.transaction_id AS transaction_id

  FROM `{BQ_PROJECT}.{BQ_DATASET}.events_*`
  WHERE _TABLE_SUFFIX BETWEEN '{bq_start}' AND '{bq_end}'
    AND event_name IN (
      'session_start', 'page_view',
      'view_item', 'add_to_cart', 'purchase'
    )
"
)

cat(sql)
```


SELECT
  -- Session / user dims
  PARSE_DATE('%Y%m%d', event_date) AS event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,
  (SELECT value.int_value
     FROM UNNEST(event_params)
    WHERE key = 'ga_session_id') AS session_id,

  -- Traffic source
  traffic_source.source AS source,
  traffic_source.medium AS medium,
  traffic_source.name AS campaign,

  -- Device
  device.category AS device_category,
  device.operating_system AS os,

  -- Geo
  geo.country AS country,

  -- Event
  event_name,
  event_timestamp,

  -- Ecommerce (NULL for non-purchase events)
  ecommerce.purchase_revenue AS revenue,
  ecommerce.transaction_id AS transaction_id

FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_*`
WHERE _TABLE_SUFFIX BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210131'
  AND event_name IN (
    'session_start', 'page_view',
    'view_item', 'add_to_cart', 'purchase'
  )

title: GA4 BigQuery Analysis with Arrow & DuckDB
text:
2c. Execute & capture as Arrow Table


Code
```{r}
#| label: fetch-arrow
#| eval: false
#| cache: true

# Execute the query; stream results into an Arrow Table
# arrow::read_ipc_stream() handles memory efficiently
result <- DBI::dbGetQuery(bq_con, sql)
```








Warning




Unfortunately, this code above led to the following error when executed.

Billed: 362.81 MB
Downloading first chunk of data.
Downloading the remaining 629,250 rows in 63 chunks of (up to) 10,000 rows.
                                                              
Warning messages:
1: In as.integer.integer64(x) : NAs produced by integer overflow
2: In as.integer.integer64(x) : NAs produced by integer overflow

Fixing the problem with requring with the following way.





Code
```{r}
# use bigrquery functions to control bigint conversion
tbl <- bigrquery::bq_project_query(GCP_PROJECT, sql) # billed 362.81 MB
result <- bigrquery::bq_table_download(
  tbl,
  bigint = "numeric",
  page_size = 10000
)
result # A tibble: 639,250 × 13
```

Code
```{r}
# Convert to Arrow Table (columnar, faster for downstream DuckDB queries)
ga4_arrow <- arrow::as_arrow_table(result) # scannable Arrow object

# Disconnect from BigQuery — we don't need it anymore
dbDisconnect(bq_con)

# Quick look
print(ga4_arrow)
```


Table
639250 rows x 13 columns
$event_date <date32[day]>
$user_pseudo_id <string>
$session_id <double>
$source <string>
$medium <string>
$campaign <string>
$device_category <string>
$os <string>
$country <string>
$event_name <string>
$event_timestamp <double>
$revenue <double>
$transaction_id <string>



Tip — very large datasets: If the dataset exceeds available RAM, write it to Parquet first and query the files directly with DuckDB:
arrow::write_parquet(ga4_arrow, "ga4_data.parquet")
# Then use duckdb::duckdb_register_arrow() or read_parquet() in SQL

title: GA4 BigQuery Analysis with Arrow & DuckDB
text:
4a. Daily sessions & revenue


Code
```{r}
#| label: daily-sessions
#| eval: true

daily <- dbGetQuery(
  duck,
  "
  SELECT
    event_date,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN event_name = 'session_start'
                        THEN user_pseudo_id || CAST(session_id AS VARCHAR)
                   END)                           AS sessions,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN event_name = 'purchase'
                        THEN transaction_id END)  AS transactions,
    ROUND(SUM(COALESCE(revenue, 0)), 2)           AS revenue
  FROM ga4
  GROUP BY event_date
  ORDER BY event_date
"
) |>
  mutate(event_date = as.Date(event_date))

head(daily)
```


  event_date sessions transactions revenue
1 2021-01-01     2289           13     975
2 2021-01-02     2705           14    1026
3 2021-01-03     2753           14     552
4 2021-01-04     3317           16     834
5 2021-01-05     4410           28    1143
6 2021-01-06     5951           31    2000


Output:
> head(daily)
  event_date sessions transactions revenue
1 2021-01-01     2289           13     975
2 2021-01-02     2705           14    1026
3 2021-01-03     2753           14     552
4 2021-01-04     3317           16     834
5 2021-01-05     4410           28    1143
6 2021-01-06     5951           31    2000


Code
```{r}
#| label: plot-daily
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 4
#| eval: true

library(scales)

ggplot(daily, aes(x = event_date)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = sessions), fill = "#4285F4", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_line(
    aes(y = revenue * max(sessions) / max(revenue)),
    color = "#EA4335",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Sessions",
    labels = comma,
    sec.axis = sec_axis(
      ~ . * max(daily$revenue) / max(daily$sessions),
      name = "Revenue (USD)",
      labels = dollar
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b %d", date_breaks = "1 week") +
  labs(title = "Daily Sessions & Revenue", x = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(axis.title.y.right = element_text(color = "#EA4335"))
```

title: GA4 BigQuery Analysis with Arrow & DuckDB
text:
4b. Channel performance


Code
```{r}
#| label: channel-performance

channels <- dbGetQuery(
  duck,
  "
  WITH sessions AS (
    SELECT
      source,
      medium,
      user_pseudo_id || CAST(session_id AS VARCHAR) AS session_key
    FROM ga4
    WHERE event_name = 'session_start'
  ),
  purchases AS (
    SELECT
      source,
      medium,
      COUNT(DISTINCT transaction_id) AS transactions,
      SUM(revenue)                   AS revenue
    FROM ga4
    WHERE event_name = 'purchase'
    GROUP BY source, medium
  )
  SELECT
    s.source,
    s.medium,
    COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_key)         AS sessions,
    COALESCE(p.transactions, 0)           AS transactions,
    ROUND(COALESCE(p.revenue, 0), 2)      AS revenue,
    ROUND(100.0 * COALESCE(p.transactions, 0)
          / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_key), 0), 2) AS cvr_pct
  FROM sessions s
  LEFT JOIN purchases p USING (source, medium)
  GROUP BY s.source, s.medium, p.transactions, p.revenue
  ORDER BY sessions DESC
  LIMIT 20
"
)

channels
```

2                        (direct)         (none)    27206          192   12284
3                         <Other>        <Other>    17580           99    5970

title: GA4 BigQuery Analysis with Arrow & DuckDB
text:
4c. Funnel analysis


Code
```{r}
#| label: funnel

all_counts <- dbGetQuery(
  duck,
  "
  SELECT
    event_name,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_pseudo_id) AS users
  FROM ga4
  GROUP BY event_name
"
)

funnel <- all_counts |>
  filter(
    event_name %in%
      c(
        "session_start",
        "page_view",
        "view_item",
        "add_to_cart",
        "purchase"
      )
  ) |>
  mutate(
    event_name = factor(
      event_name,
      levels = c(
        "session_start",
        "page_view",
        "view_item",
        "add_to_cart",
        "purchase"
      )
    ),
    step_pct = round(100 * users / max(users), 1)
  ) |>
  arrange(event_name)

funnel
```


     event_name users step_pct
1 session_start 93552     98.9
2     page_view 94630    100.0
3     view_item 19629     20.7
4   add_to_cart  3832      4.0
5      purchase  1069      1.1




Code
```{r}
#| label: plot-funnel
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4

ggplot(funnel, aes(x = event_name, y = users, fill = event_name)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = glue("{comma(users)}\n({step_pct}%)")),
    vjust = -0.3,
    size = 3.5
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues", direction = -1) +
  labs(title = "GA4 Event Funnel", x = NULL, y = "Unique Users") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

title: GA4 BigQuery Analysis with Arrow & DuckDB
text:
4d. Device breakdown


Code
```{r}
#| label: device-breakdown

devices <- dbGetQuery(
  duck,
  "
  SELECT
    device_category,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_pseudo_id || CAST(session_id AS VARCHAR)) AS sessions,
    ROUND(SUM(COALESCE(revenue,0)), 2)  AS revenue
  FROM ga4
  GROUP BY device_category
  ORDER BY sessions DESC
"
)
```
